---
title: "Replication and Where Week 6 Goes"
date: "2026-07-27"
---

# Replication and Where Week 6 Goes {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter six-point-two, part nine of nine — the last part. In part eight we mapped the paper's vulnerabilities. Now we make the magic mechanical: three replication exercises you run in notebook six-point-two, then where this chapter connects and what to ask next.
:::

## Exercise 1: Rediscover An Industry

- Take 15–20 well-known firms across a few industries
- Two oil majors, two airlines, two banks, two streamers
- Tokenize, drop stop-words, build a TF–IDF matrix
- Compute the full pairwise cosine matrix
- Industry blocks light up — *with no labels given*

::: notes
These run in notebook six-point-two, which hands you a small corpus of product descriptions and the tooling to vectorize them. The goal is not the full T-N-I-C library — that is a months-long pipeline — but a mini version on a handful of firms. Exercise one: build a mini cosine-similarity measure and rediscover an industry. Take product descriptions for fifteen to twenty well-known firms across a few obvious industries — two oil majors, two airlines, two banks, two streamers, two automakers. Tokenize, drop stop-words, build a T-F-I-D-F matrix, and compute the full pairwise cosine matrix. You should reproduce the core intuition with your own code: the two oil firms score near each other, the two airlines near each other, an oil firm and an airline low — without ever telling the code what an industry is. Print the matrix as a heatmap and watch the industry blocks light up on the diagonal.
:::

## Exercises 2 And 3: Knobs And Networks

- **Exercise 2 — knobs are levers**: change one choice at a time
- Plain counts pull *everyone* together; bad stop-words fake similarity
- Move the threshold and a competitor count balloons or collapses
- **Exercise 3 — networks**: find a firm straddling two businesses
- Show **intransitivity**, then recompute years to show **time-variation**

::: notes
Exercise two: stress the construction and show the measure depends on your choices. Re-run exercise one changing one knob at a time. Swap T-F-I-D-F for plain counts and watch "company" and "products" pull everyone toward similarity, showing why the inverse-document-frequency reweighting matters. Change the stop-word list, or fail to strip proper nouns, and show two firms scoring similar merely for both naming the same state. Move the similarity threshold and watch a firm's competitor count balloon or collapse. The lesson made tactile: knobs are levers, and a measurement paper's claims are only as stable as the choices behind them. Exercise three: extend to the network properties S-I-C cannot represent. Pick a firm straddling two businesses — an Amazon-like firm or a conglomerate — and show with your scores that it neighbors firms in two blocks that are not neighbors of each other: intransitivity on your own data. Then, if the notebook supplies two years of filings for a firm that changed its business, recompute its neighborhood each year and show it moved: time-variation.
:::

## Where This Connects

- **Chapter 1.2**: correlation is the cosine — pure geometry
- Here the same cosine, on word vectors, redraws American industry
- **Chapter 6.3 (Loughran–McDonald)**: same text, asks *tone*
- **Chapter 6.5 (AI Co-Pilot)**: bag-of-words → learned embeddings
- Week 6 is one long argument: turn words into numbers *responsibly*

::: notes
Where does this connect? Chapter one-point-two said correlation is the cosine of an angle between two vectors — pure geometry. Here the same cosine, pointed at word vectors, redraws the map of American industry. It sets up the rest of Week Six. Chapter six-point-three, Loughran and McDonald, stays in the bag-of-words world but asks a different question of the same text — tone, via finance-specific word lists. And Chapter six-point-five, the A-I Co-Pilot, is where bag-of-words gives way to learned embeddings that capture meaning, answering this paper's "bag-of-words ignores meaning" vulnerability. Read in order, Week Six is one long argument about turning words into numbers responsibly. Go now to notebook six-point-two and do exercise one. The instant your own code scores two oil firms as near-twins and an oil firm and an airline as strangers — with you having defined no industries at all — the abstraction collapses into something you know, and you never again read a ten-K as merely a legal document.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Build a mini cosine measure and watch industries emerge with **no labels** (Exercise 1); turn the knobs to see they are levers (Exercise 2); reproduce **intransitivity and time-variation** SIC cannot (Exercise 3). Week 6 is one argument: turn words into numbers responsibly — next, **Loughran–McDonald** on tone.
:::

::: notes
To summarize this final part: notebook six-point-two lets you build a mini cosine measure and watch industry blocks emerge with no labels at all; turn the knobs and see they are levers; and reproduce the intransitivity and time-variation that S-I-C cannot. That closes our reader's guide to Hoberg and Phillips. Week Six is one long argument about turning words into numbers responsibly — and next, in Chapter six-point-three, Loughran and McDonald point the same text at a new question: tone.
:::
